input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising components such as permanent magnet synchronous motors, gear transmissions, and cycloidal gears. This robotic system operates in a manufacturing environment where precise motion control and high accuracy are critical, making it suitable for digital twinning due to its complex dynamics and need for real-time monitoring and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for system complexity management. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is handled via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed system behavior.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol, and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are implemented for system integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: SysML-based structural models of the robot axis, Simulink friction models, and data traces such as I_M (motor current) and r_M (motor position) from the MABI robot, utilizing RFLP methodology for hierarchical abstraction across requirements, functions, logic, and physics layers, integrated with MBSE tools like Simulink and SysML IBDs for component and connector perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, controlled via Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl with position, speed, or acceleration inputs, enabling precise actuation and motion control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Two position encoders per drive: motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder behind the gearbox, providing high-resolution feedback for positional accuracy and backlash reduction.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Uncertainty sources include joint friction, load variations, and temperature effects; validation via comparison of simulated data against measured data to quantify deviations, with sensor arrangements reducing positional uncertainty and backlash.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Automated one-way data flow from physical to digital where time series of sensor data, specifically input current and output angle, are streamed via MQTT; reference data is partly accessible from the machine and partly derived.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT communication protocol with publish-subscribe topology for data streaming between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses a development phase utilizing Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) and methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), transitioning to an operational phase where continuous model updates occur based on real-time data from physical assets like robot axes during operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows four specific steps: 1) Problem identification, 2) Asset and model analysis, 3) Use or creation of calculations or models, and 4) Data identification. Evolution of the Digital Twin involves continuous improvement of white-box and black-box friction models during operation, addressing current research gaps in real-time model refinement for enhanced accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Insights are generated through analysis of friction loss relations in the robot’s first axis, supported by live-data linking and dashboard visualization to enhance decision-making accuracy and operational efficiency.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The system includes a physical safety cell that restricts the maximum working range to ensure safe operation.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the physical system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and lists its key components ('permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', 'cycloidal gear') as found in the use case section. It correctly identifies the operational domain (manufacturing/machining) and the suitability for digital twinning based on the paper's focus on modeling for accuracy.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. However, the Source Documents explicitly describe services provided by the digital shadow/twin, such as 'understanding of joint friction', 'improve robot motion accuracy', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification', and 'visualization in a dashboard' (Abstract, Section IV). Therefore, the claim that this information is not in the document is incorrect.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by Section II.A ('Modeling Software and Systems'). It correctly lists MBSE methodologies: 'RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT' and ADLs: 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs'. It also correctly identifies behavior modeling tools like 'MontiArc Automaton' and 'state machines in SysML v1.7', as well as simulation tools: 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time, data-driven representations' and data 'streamed via MQTT', but it does not provide the specific technical details required by the definition, such as synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs.

The document describes a single instance called 'FrictionShadow' for the first axis of the robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins.

The document explicitly differentiates between a 'Digital Shadow' and a 'Digital Twin', defining the shadow as a 'passive entity' and the twin as having 'active components'. Since the paper focuses solely on the implementation of a digital shadow architecture, the overall orchestration and coordination of a Digital Twin system are not described.

The extraction mentions 'Sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol, and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are implemented for system integration.' The evidence supports this: the text states 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT' and mentions 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' in the abstract. However, the extraction lacks depth regarding interoperability standards or integration with external systems/enterprise systems beyond the implementation of AAS and MQTT. It is specific and correct but could be expanded.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the automated one-way data flow described in the text. It correctly identifies the specific data types (input current and output angle), the communication mechanism (streamed via MQTT), and the origin of reference data (partly accessible, partly derived). Quotes: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.', 'The reference data is partly accessible from the machine and partly derived.'

The source documents define the digital shadow as a passive entity that does not push data back to the physical system. Consequently, there are no control flows, command types, or feedback mechanisms to describe. The extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate regarding the technical details of the interaction.

The extraction correctly describes MQTT as the communication protocol and its publish-subscribe topology for data streaming. The source text supports this: 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' However, the extraction could be expanded with more details on network infrastructure, bandwidth, latency, or security, which are not present in the text. The description is specific and well-supported for the available information.

The source documents mention implementation tools like Simulink and a dashboard, as well as the use of Asset Administration Shells, but do not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), deployment models, or infrastructure requirements. The extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.

It correctly identifies MBSE, SysML, the specific abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), and the RFLP methodology. The description accurately lists the four steps of the methodology explicitly outlined in Section II.C and applied in Section IV.A. The extraction is specific to the use case, correctly identifying the generation of insights through the analysis of friction loss relations in the robot's first axis. The extraction states 'Not in Document,' which is correct. No relevant evidence is present.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. A review of the source text confirms that there is no mention of data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, consent management, or data anonymization. The document focuses exclusively on digital shadows, friction modeling, and the technical architecture of the robot system.

The extraction accurately identifies a specific safety feature of the MABI MAX robot described in the use case section. It notes the physical safety cell and its restriction of the working range. Supported by quote: 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range to [−90[◦], 90[◦]]'.
